program.input: import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.integrate import dblquad, tplquad, solve_ivp

subsection.title: Grid for a function of two variables
subsection.p: For z=f(x,y), make arrays of x- and y-values:

program.input: x = np.linspace(a, b, 200) y = np.linspace(c, d, 200) X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y) Z = f(X, Y)

subsection.p: Here X, Y, and Z are arrays. The function f must accept arrays.

program.input:
fig = plt.figure() ax = fig.add_subplot(projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z)
ax.set_xlabel("x") ax.set_ylabel("y") ax.set_zlabel("z") plt.show()

subsection.title: Wireframe plot

program.input:
fig = plt.figure() ax = fig.add_subplot(projection="3d")
ax.plot_wireframe(X, Y, Z)
plt.show()

subsection.p: A wireframe is often easier to read than a solid surface.

subsection.title: Contour plot
subsection.p: A contour plot shows level curves: f(x,y)=c.

program.input: plt.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=20) plt.xlabel("x") plt.ylabel("y") plt.axis("equal") plt.show()
plt.contourf(X, Y, Z, levels=30) plt.colorbar() plt.axis("equal") plt.show()

subsection.title: Implicit curves
subsection.p: To plot F(x,y)=0, plot the zero contour:

program.input: Z = F(X, Y) plt.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=[0]) plt.axis("equal") plt.show()

subsection.title: Plotting a parametrized surface
subsection.p: For \mathbf r(u,v)=(x(u,v),y(u,v),z(u,v)), use a parameter grid:

program.input:
u = np.linspace(u0, u1, 150) v = np.linspace(v0, v1, 150) U, V = np.meshgrid(u, v)
X = x(U, V) Y = y(U, V) Z = z(U, V)
fig = plt.figure() ax = fig.add_subplot(projection="3d") ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z) plt.show()

theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 150) z = np.linspace(z0, z1, 100) Theta, Z = np.meshgrid(theta, z)
X = R*np.cos(Theta) Y = R*np.sin(Theta)
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z)

phi = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 150) theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 150) Phi, Theta = np.meshgrid(phi, theta)
X = R*np.sin(Phi)*np.cos(Theta) Y = R*np.sin(Phi)*np.sin(Theta) Z = R*np.cos(Phi)
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z)

row.cell: Choose a domain large enough to show the important feature.
Use enough grid points to avoid jagged artifacts.
Check whether the plot hides singularities or holes.

warning.p: A computer plot is a sample, not a proof.
A surface may look smooth because the grid is too coarse.
A contour may disappear if the requested level is outside the range of plotted values.
A 3-D plot may distort scale unless the axes are adjusted.
